refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the ability to measure and report energy efficiency metrics, and (2) the achievement of a target value. The latter is a performance constraint or acceptance criterion, not a functional requirement of the system itself. The system's functional role is to provide visibility into energy consumption and work output so that stakeholders can assess performance against targets. Achievement of the target is a property of the overall system design and operational execution, not a function the system 'supports' in isolation. This refinement keeps the FR in the problem domain (what must be measured and communicated) and removes the solution-dependent notion of 'support achieving,' which is vague and outcome-focused rather than capability-focused.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verify that the NGHE solution collects, calculates, and reports energy_per_ton = energy_used / ton_moved with defined accuracy, granularity (e.g., per shift, per task, per equipment unit), and accessibility to authorized stakeholders (Site Manager, Safety Manager, Maintenance/Quality) over the agreed evaluation period. Confirm that reported values are traceable to underlying sensor data and work-output logs.
sub_frs: The NGHE solution shall measure and log energy consumed by the equipment during operation.
The NGHE solution shall measure and log the quantity of work output (e.g., mass moved, distance traveled, or task-specific units) during operation.
The NGHE solution shall calculate energy efficiency metrics (energy per unit work output) from logged energy and work-output data.
The NGHE solution shall provide authorized stakeholders access to energy efficiency metrics and underlying measurement data for analysis and reporting.

candidate_dps: DP1: Instrument energy input (fuel/electricity) at source; instrument work output (tonnage moved) at point of delivery; compute ratio offline post-evaluation period
DP2: Deploy real-time energy and tonnage sensors with continuous data aggregation; compute energy_per_ton at configurable intervals; expose metric via reporting interface

DP3: Integrate energy metering into power distribution subsystem; integrate tonnage measurement into load/transport subsystem; synchronize timestamps and compute ratio via independent analytics module

DP4: Establish energy accounting via fuel consumption logs and equipment runtime; establish work accounting via haul tickets and manifest records; reconcile and compute ratio through manual or semi-automated post-processing
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP3: Integrate energy metering into power distribution subsystem; integrate tonnage measurement into load/transport subsystem; synchronize timestamps and compute ratio via independent analytics module

potential_sources: Energy metering and tonnage measurement subsystems remain logically decoupled (separate instrumentation domains); coupling occurs only at the analytics module boundary via standardized data exchange (timestamp, energy_joules, tonnage_kg)
Risk: If energy and tonnage sensors lack synchronized clocks, ratio computation becomes ambiguous during transient periods; mitigation: require NTP or GPS time synchronization at sensor level

Risk: Analytics module depends on both subsystems producing data; if either fails, metric becomes unavailable; mitigation: design analytics to handle missing data gracefully and flag data quality issues
coupling_check.notes: DP3 achieves separation of concerns: measurement logic is isolated from computation logic. Energy subsystem owns metering; transport subsystem owns tonnage; analytics owns aggregation and ratio. This supports independent testing, evolution, and operational ownership.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Energy source is centralized or metered at a single point (e.g., fuel depot, main electrical panel). Risk: If equipment draws energy from multiple sources (e.g., on-site generator + grid), metering must cover all sources or ratio will be incomplete. Mitigation: define energy boundary explicitly in worksite plan.

Assumption: Tonnage measurement is feasible at point of delivery (e.g., weighbridge, load cell on transport vehicle). Risk: If work output is defined differently (e.g., distance moved, volume excavated), FR must be revised. Mitigation: confirm acceptance criteria definition with stakeholders.

Assumption: Evaluation period is fixed and known in advance. Risk: If period is dynamic or overlaps multiple operational shifts, timestamp synchronization and data windowing become complex. Mitigation: establish clear evaluation period boundaries and clock synchronization protocol.

Assumption: Worksite safety plan permits installation of metering hardware on energy and transport subsystems. Risk: If safety constraints prohibit intrusive instrumentation, non-contact or indirect measurement methods may be required. Mitigation: engage safety review early.

Assumption: Data quality (sensor accuracy, calibration, drift) is acceptable for decision-making. Risk: Uncalibrated or drifting sensors will produce misleading ratios. Mitigation: define sensor accuracy requirements and calibration schedule.

Assumption: Analytics module can be deployed independently of energy and transport subsystems. Risk: If subsystems do not expose data interfaces, analytics cannot function. Mitigation: require data export/API contracts from both subsystems.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR should be refined to explicitly state: (1) Energy boundary definition (which sources are included in 'energy_used'); (2) Work output definition (tonnage moved from point A to point B, or equivalent); (3) Evaluation period duration and synchronization requirements; (4) Acceptable sensor accuracy and calibration frequency; (5) Data availability and latency requirements for reporting. Additionally, confirm that DP3's assumption of independent subsystem instrumentation is compatible with worksite safety and operational policies. If subsystems cannot be instrumented independently, consider DP2 (centralized real-time sensor deployment) as fallback, accepting tighter coupling in exchange for simpler integration.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized analytics and metering integration hub that aggregates energy metering from power distribution subsystem, tonnage measurement from load/transport subsystem, synchronizes timestamps across distributed sensors, and computes efficiency ratios independent of real-time vehicle control loops.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives energy metering data from Vehicle power distribution subsystem and tonnage/load data from Vehicle transport subsystem; receives synchronized timestamp metadata.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Outputs computed efficiency ratios (energy-per-tonnage metrics) and analytics results back to Vehicle and Teleoperation for monitoring and optimization.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communicates with distributed Vehicle instances to collect metering and measurement data; may operate on-vehicle or cloud-based depending on deployment architecture.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent containment: NGHE Solution is explicitly positioned as the analytics and integration layer. Energy metering integration, tonnage measurement integration, timestamp synchronization, and ratio computation are all analytics functions naturally owned by this module. Minimal diffusion: only Telemetry interface leakage to Vehicle subsystems for data ingestion.

Poor containment: Vehicle owns power distribution and load/transport subsystems but should not own independent analytics logic. Timestamp synchronization and ratio computation would diffuse across multiple vehicle subsystems (power, transport, control). Creates tight coupling between real-time vehicle control and analytics, violating separation of concerns.

Weak containment: Teleoperation is a user-facing interface layer, not an analytics engine. Placing metering integration and ratio computation here would couple operator control logic with backend analytics, creating high diffusion across Vehicle and Safety Services for data sourcing.

Very poor containment: Safety Services is specialized for hazard detection and safety alerts. Energy efficiency analytics is orthogonal to safety concerns. High diffusion: would require Safety Services to depend on Vehicle power/transport subsystems and Teleoperation for data, violating single responsibility.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The DP is fundamentally an analytics and data integration function that sits above the Vehicle subsystems. NGHE Solution is the designated analytics and integration layer in the architecture. By centralizing energy metering integration, tonnage measurement integration, timestamp synchronization, and ratio computation within NGHE Solution, we achieve high cohesion (all analytics logic in one place) and low coupling (Vehicle subsystems remain independent; NGHE Solution pulls data via Telemetry interface without modifying vehicle control logic).
containment_risks: Risk: If NGHE Solution is deployed on-vehicle (embedded), timestamp synchronization may require tight coupling to vehicle real-time clocks, increasing latency sensitivity.
Risk: If energy metering or tonnage measurement data is not already exposed via Telemetry interface, Vehicle subsystems must be modified to emit these signals, creating secondary diffusion.
Risk: Ratio computation may require near-real-time latency; if NGHE Solution is cloud-based, Network Link latency could degrade analytics freshness.
Risk: Multiple Vehicle instances reporting to NGHE Solution creates a fan-in dependency; if NGHE Solution fails, analytics are unavailable across the fleet.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP3 directly addresses the FR by specifying a complete solution architecture: (1) energy metering integration into power distribution, (2) tonnage measurement integration into load/transport, (3) timestamp synchronization across distributed sensors, and (4) independent ratio computation. The DP is solution-neutral (does not prescribe implementation technology) and logically sufficient to enable the required measurement and reporting capability. The separation of analytics from real-time vehicle control is sound.

diffusion_risks: Secondary diffusion risk: Energy metering and tonnage measurement data must already be exposed via Telemetry interface from Vehicle subsystems. If these signals are not currently available, Vehicle power distribution and load/transport subsystems must be modified to emit them, creating unplanned coupling and scope creep.

Deployment latency risk: If NGHE Solution is cloud-based, Network Link latency may degrade analytics freshness and violate real-time reporting requirements. If deployed on-vehicle, timestamp synchronization may require tight coupling to vehicle real-time clocks, increasing complexity and latency sensitivity.

Single point of failure: Multiple Vehicle instances reporting to a centralized NGHE Solution creates a fan-in dependency. Failure of NGHE Solution disables analytics across the entire fleet, violating operational resilience.

Data schema coupling: Telemetry interface must define strict contracts for energy metering format, tonnage measurement units, and timestamp metadata. Changes to Vehicle subsystem data schemas will propagate to NGHE Solution, creating hidden coupling.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution achieves excellent containment (0.92 score) as the designated analytics and integration layer. All four DP responsibilities (metering integration, tonnage integration, timestamp synchronization, ratio computation) are naturally owned by this module. Minimal diffusion to Vehicle subsystems is limited to data ingestion via Telemetry interface, preserving separation of concerns. Vehicle subsystems remain independent; NGHE Solution pulls data without modifying vehicle control logic. However, containment is contingent on: (1) Telemetry interface already exposing required energy and tonnage signals, and (2) acceptable latency and availability characteristics of the chosen deployment topology (on-vehicle vs. cloud).

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Acceptance criterion 'energy_per_ton ≤ E_target' is directly verifiable via NGHE Solution outputs. The module computes the ratio from integrated energy metering and tonnage measurement data, producing a measurable metric. Verification requires: (1) calibration and validation of energy metering and tonnage sensors, (2) confirmation that timestamp synchronization achieves required precision (recommend ±100ms or better), and (3) definition of evaluation period and aggregation window (e.g., per-shift, per-job, per-fleet-day). Worksite safety plan and operational policies are external constraints that do not directly depend on NGHE Solution architecture; however, NGHE Solution must not interfere with safety-critical vehicle control loops (satisfied by independent analytics module design).
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: NGHE Solution owns analytics and integration; Vehicle subsystems own metering and measurement hardware/sensors; Telemetry interface owns data transport contracts. However, allocation clarity is weakened by ambiguity in: (1) which subsystem owns Telemetry interface definition and evolution (Vehicle, NGHE Solution, or shared?), (2) whether energy metering and tonnage measurement are already instrumented in Vehicle subsystems or require new hardware/firmware, and (3) deployment topology decision (on-vehicle vs. cloud) and its impact on latency and availability SLAs.

recommended_revisions: Revise DP3 to explicitly state: 'Telemetry interface shall expose energy metering data (units: kWh, precision: ±2%) and tonnage measurement data (units: metric tons, precision: ±5%) from Vehicle subsystems with synchronized timestamps (precision: ±100ms). NGHE Solution shall compute energy_per_ton ratio and report results with latency ≤ [X seconds] and availability ≥ [Y%].' This clarifies data contracts and performance requirements.

Add secondary DP: 'DP3a: Instrument Vehicle power distribution subsystem with energy metering sensor (if not already present) and expose via Telemetry interface. Instrument Vehicle load/transport subsystem with tonnage measurement sensor (if not already present) and expose via Telemetry interface.' This makes sensor instrumentation explicit and prevents hidden scope creep.

Add deployment decision gate: 'DP3b: Select NGHE Solution deployment topology (on-vehicle embedded vs. cloud-based) based on latency, availability, and cost constraints. If on-vehicle, define timestamp synchronization mechanism (e.g., GPS, NTP, vehicle CAN bus). If cloud-based, define Network Link SLA (latency, bandwidth, availability) and fallback behavior during connectivity loss.' This mitigates latency and resilience risks.

Add resilience requirement: 'NGHE Solution shall implement redundancy or graceful degradation to maintain analytics availability if a single Vehicle instance fails to report. Consider distributed analytics (per-vehicle) vs. centralized aggregation (fleet-wide) trade-off.' This addresses single point of failure risk.

Clarify Telemetry interface ownership: 'Vehicle subsystems shall own metering/measurement sensor hardware and firmware. NGHE Solution shall own analytics logic and ratio computation. Telemetry interface shall be jointly owned and versioned; breaking changes require coordination between Vehicle and NGHE Solution teams.' This prevents diffusion and clarifies governance.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass, but the gate results expose critical ambiguities and contingencies that must be resolved before proceeding to sub-FR decomposition. The DP-IM gate identifies five concrete diffusion and deployment risks that are contingent on unresolved architectural decisions: (1) whether energy metering and tonnage measurement sensors are already instrumented in Vehicle subsystems, (2) whether Telemetry interface currently exposes these signals, (3) deployment topology choice (on-vehicle vs. cloud) and its latency/availability implications, (4) timestamp synchronization mechanism and precision requirements, and (5) resilience strategy for centralized analytics. The FR-IM gate notes that allocation clarity is weakened by ambiguity in Telemetry interface ownership and deployment SLAs. These are not minor details—they directly affect whether the current DP is realizable without unplanned scope creep, latency violations, or single-point-of-failure risks. Proceeding to sub-FR decomposition without resolving these contingencies risks propagating ambiguity downward and creating rework. The recommended revisions in the gate output provide a clear path: revise DP3 to make data contracts, performance SLAs, and deployment decisions explicit, and add secondary DPs (DP3a, DP3b) to surface sensor instrumentation and deployment topology as explicit design choices. This transforms contingencies into explicit design parameters that can then be decomposed into sub-FRs with clarity.

revise.why: DP3 must be revised to explicitly specify: (1) Telemetry interface data contracts (energy metering units, precision, tonnage measurement units, precision, timestamp precision ±100ms), (2) NGHE Solution performance SLAs (computation latency ≤ X seconds, availability ≥ Y%), (3) deployment topology decision (on-vehicle embedded vs. cloud-based) with corresponding timestamp synchronization mechanism, and (4) resilience strategy (redundancy or graceful degradation for analytics availability). Additionally, introduce secondary DPs: DP3a to address sensor instrumentation (energy metering in power distribution, tonnage measurement in load/transport) as explicit design choices, and DP3b to formalize deployment topology and Network Link SLA requirements. These revisions convert the five identified diffusion risks and contingencies into explicit, analyzable design parameters that can be traced to sub-FRs and verified against acceptance criteria.

assumptions: Energy metering and tonnage measurement sensors are either already instrumented in Vehicle subsystems or can be added without violating vehicle safety-critical control loops.
Telemetry interface is either already defined and exposed by Vehicle subsystems or can be extended to include energy metering and tonnage measurement signals without breaking existing consumers.
NGHE Solution deployment topology (on-vehicle vs. cloud) is a design choice that can be made independently of Vehicle subsystem architecture.
Timestamp synchronization precision of ±100ms is acceptable for energy-per-ton ratio reporting; tighter precision (e.g., ±10ms) is not required by worksite safety or operational policies.

Centralized NGHE Solution analytics (fan-in from multiple Vehicle instances) is acceptable from a resilience perspective, or fallback/degradation mechanisms can be implemented to mitigate single-point-of-failure risk.

Data schema contracts for Telemetry interface (energy metering format, tonnage measurement units, timestamp metadata) can be jointly owned and versioned by Vehicle and NGHE Solution teams without creating unmanageable coupling.
open_risks: Scope creep risk: If energy metering and tonnage measurement sensors are not currently instrumented in Vehicle subsystems, adding them may require hardware redesign, firmware updates, and validation against vehicle safety standards, expanding scope beyond NGHE Solution analytics.

Latency risk: If NGHE Solution is cloud-based, Network Link latency may degrade analytics freshness and violate real-time reporting requirements. If on-vehicle, timestamp synchronization may require tight coupling to vehicle real-time clocks, increasing complexity.

Resilience risk: Centralized NGHE Solution creates a single point of failure for fleet-wide analytics. Failure disables energy-per-ton reporting across all Vehicle instances, potentially violating operational resilience SLAs.

Data schema coupling risk: Telemetry interface must define strict contracts for energy metering format, tonnage measurement units, and timestamp metadata. Changes to Vehicle subsystem data schemas will propagate to NGHE Solution, creating hidden coupling and rework.

Telemetry interface ownership ambiguity: Unclear which team (Vehicle, NGHE Solution, or shared) owns Telemetry interface definition, evolution, and versioning. This may lead to coordination failures and diffusion of responsibility.

Sensor instrumentation ambiguity: Unclear whether energy metering and tonnage measurement are already available in Vehicle subsystems or require new hardware/firmware. This ambiguity prevents accurate scope and risk assessment.
evidence_needed: Audit of Vehicle subsystems to confirm whether energy metering sensor is already instrumented in power distribution subsystem and exposed via Telemetry interface. If not, estimate effort and risk to add.

Audit of Vehicle subsystems to confirm whether tonnage measurement sensor is already instrumented in load/transport subsystem and exposed via Telemetry interface. If not, estimate effort and risk to add.

Definition of Telemetry interface data contracts: energy metering (units, precision, sampling rate), tonnage measurement (units, precision, sampling rate), timestamp metadata (format, precision, synchronization mechanism).

Deployment topology decision: on-vehicle embedded vs. cloud-based. If on-vehicle, specify timestamp synchronization mechanism (GPS, NTP, vehicle CAN bus) and latency budget. If cloud-based, specify Network Link SLA (latency, bandwidth, availability) and fallback behavior during connectivity loss.
Performance SLA definition: NGHE Solution computation latency (target: ≤ X seconds), availability (target: ≥ Y%), and aggregation window (per-shift, per-job, per-fleet-day).

Resilience strategy: Specify redundancy or graceful degradation mechanism to maintain analytics availability if a single Vehicle instance fails to report. Evaluate distributed analytics (per-vehicle) vs. centralized aggregation (fleet-wide) trade-off.

Telemetry interface ownership and governance model: Clarify which team owns interface definition, evolution, and versioning. Define process for breaking changes and coordination between Vehicle and NGHE Solution teams.

Verification plan: Define calibration and validation procedures for energy metering and tonnage sensors, timestamp synchronization precision validation (recommend ±100ms or better), and acceptance test criteria for energy_per_ton ratio reporting.
